front: Mural: located at Dartmouth College's Baker Memorial Library; twenty panels chronicling native migration, European arrival, and rise of industry
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: Gods of the Modern World section — skeletons in mortarboards and gowns watch a skeleton give birth atop a pile of books; fetus skeletons in glass jars represent the birth of useless knowledge
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: Departure of Quetzalcoatl section — black serpents rise from the sea around a white-robed, golden-bearded figure who points toward a red pyramid
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: Cortez section — conquistadors wielding a cross like a weapon; ornately armored horse rears behind a knight carrying a giant metal cross
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: The Machine section — giant black pillar devours a pile of bodies; gray-cloaked figure hugs cross behind armored swordsman
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: a blue-clad woman surrounded by rural children is juxtaposed with the industrial black pillar called 'The Machine'
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: a decorated general prepares to stab Emiliano Zapata in the back
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: a horde of stern, identical-looking blonde children are depicted near a barnhouse; three sections on Modern Industrial Man
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: Ernest Hopkins supported the commission; Hood Museum maintains a digital version
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: Jackson Pollock visited this mural and was inspired to paint the figurative Bald Woman with Skeleton
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: Jacquelynn Baas posited that a section depicting Christ destroying his cross symbolizes the artist's denial of a sacrificial destiny as a Mexican citizen
back: The Epic of American Civilization (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: title figure is on his knees, reaching beyond the top of the painting to claim fire; located in Frary Dining Hall at Pomona College
back: Prometheus mural (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Mural: a side panel depicts black-and-white centaurs watching Prometheus steal fire
back: Prometheus mural (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Artist: Sergei Eisenstein wrote an essay comparing his films to this Mexican muralist's style, calling the essay 'Prometheus of Mexican Painting'
back: José Clemente Orozco

front: Mural: 340 murals in the former Cabañas Hospice (orphanage) in Guadalajara; the hospice stands opposite the Degollado Theatre on Tapatia Plaza
back: Cabañas Hospice Murals (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Painting: Christ uses an axe to destroy his own cross; surrounded by a Greek column, a statue of Buddha, and a pile of guns
back: Christ Destroying His Cross (José Clemente Orozco)

front: Artist: one of 'los tres grandes' of the Mexican Mural Renaissance, alongside Diego Rivera and David Alfaro Siqueiros
back: José Clemente Orozco

front: Artist: taught by the pseudonymous Dr. Atl; early career included House of Tears watercolors of prostitutes and cartoons for the newspaper The Vanguard
back: José Clemente Orozco

front: Artist: shares surname with contemporary conceptual artist who created the skull sculpture Black Kites and the chess installation Horses Running Endlessly
back: José Clemente Orozco (cf. Gabriel Orozco)

front: Artist: Ecuadorian muralist Guayasamin studied alongside this artist; both met at a gallery hosted by the Rockefeller family
back: José Clemente Orozco

front: Painting: The Trench — a blonde man surrounded by waves points angrily out to sea as gray people look on; shirtless men with machine gun rounds smashed against rocks
back: The Trench (José Clemente Orozco)
